verdict.visual_pleasure: Newsreader serif on warm paper at a disciplined 34rem measure, generous chapter openers with drop caps, rationed pull-quotes drawn verbatim from Orwell, and grainy charcoal-noir openers create a genuinely inviting long-read. Density is well-paced — one image per view, never crowding the prose — and the dark-mode tokens are properly tuned. I kept scrolling willingly.
verdict.navigation: Exemplary. Header pips mark all 15 views, the summoned overlay TOC opens on 't' with full arrow/Esc keyboard handling and focus management, a within-chapter scroll bar tracks reading position, and the 'Continue to Chapter N' footer button names the next chapter's title. Cadence (4/6/3/1 across the Parts plus solo views for set-pieces) matches the novel's dramatic rhythm. Nothing distracts; everything serves reading.
verdict.comprehension: Answered all 10 comprehension questions correctly from the HTML alone. Every target beat — Big Brother slogan, telescreen, the diary thoughtcrime, the Julia embrace, Charrington's room, Goldstein on the purpose of war, the interrogation, Room 101 and the rats, the Chestnut Tree ending, and Newspeak's purpose — is present in verbatim Orwell anchors, not paraphrase. The bridges only stitch; they never carry the load.
verdict.illustration: The noir openers set dread without spoiling (e.g. the raid image arrives at the chapter it depicts, not before), and all four structural diagrams — four Ministries, Party pyramid with percentages, three super-states in perpetual war, Newspeak A/B/C vocabulary — land exactly where Orwell's text introduces the concept and genuinely aid the abstract sections. Marked 4 not 5 only because the diagrams are functional-plain mermaid boxes and a couple of figcaptions read awkwardly ('Illustrating ch...' grammar glitch); they inform but don't delight.
verdict.books_finished_rate: At ~155 minutes / 34k words it preserves enough verbatim Orwell — including the full weight of the Goldstein and O'Brien set-pieces — that I'd finish it in one or two sittings and feel I truly knew the book, where the 1949 original would sit unfinished; it clears the bar for replacing the original on plot, themes, and key passages, though a purist would still want the original for the prose I didn't see at 32.6% coverage.
